title: Palets Bretons
subtitle: Individual Breton butter biscuits with salted crunch
excerpt: Palets Bretons are the individual biscuit form of Breton butter baking: thick, round, deeply flavoured with butter and salt, with a slight crunch on the outside and a short, sandy crumb inside. They are closer to a thick sablé than to an English biscuit; denser and more cake-like than a digestive, more robust than a shortbread. The dough is formed into a cylinder, sliced, and baked in bun tins or rings so the sides stay straight. They keep well for a week.
sourceNotes: Palets Bretons are the individual-biscuit version of the galette bretonne, the large butter cake of Brittany. They have been sold in Breton bakeries and confectioners since the mid-nineteenth century and are now a widely recognised regional speciality. The name means Breton discs or pucks, after their shape. Biscuiteries in Pont-Aven and Quimper have produced them commercially since the 1850s.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight tin for up to 1 week. The flavour improves after the first day.

content.text: The dough is made by creaming rather than rubbing, giving a slightly more cake-like texture than a rubbed shortcrust. Form it into a cylinder, refrigerate until firm, then slice into rounds. If you have a bun tin with shallow hollows, bake the rounds in those; the sides stay perfectly vertical and the biscuits are the same diameter top and bottom. Without a tin, they spread slightly but still bake correctly. The crosshatch scoring with a fork is traditional.

items.prepNote: beaten with 1 tsp water, for egg wash

content.text: Beat 200 g softened butter with 100 g icing sugar until pale and creamy. Beat in 3 egg yolks, 1 tsp salt, and 0.5 tsp vanilla. Add 220 g flour and mix until just combined. Form the dough into a cylinder about 5 cm in diameter, wrap tightly in cling film, and refrigerate for at least 60 minutes until firm. Heat the oven to 170 degrees C fan. Slice the cylinder into rounds about 1 cm thick. Place in the hollows of a lightly greased bun tin, or space well apart on a lined baking tray. Brush with the egg yolk wash. Score a crosshatch across each biscuit with the back of a fork. Bake for 16 to 18 minutes until deep golden; they should be uniformly amber, not just at the edges. Cool completely on a rack before storing.

items.problem: Biscuits spread and are very thin
items.solution: The dough was too warm when sliced and baked. Refrigerate the cylinder until it is very firm — you should be able to slice cleanly without it squashing. If the kitchen is warm, rest the sliced rounds on the tray in the fridge for 10 minutes before baking.

items.problem: Very pale and soft after the full bake time
items.solution: Palets Bretons need to reach a deep amber to develop their characteristic flavour and crunch. Pale biscuits will be soft and bland. Bake until clearly golden — not just at the edges but across the whole surface.

This is the character of the biscuit. The salt level is deliberately generous, in keeping with the Breton tradition of salted butter baking. Reduce to 0.75 tsp for a milder version, but the full amount is traditional.
